scenarioName: Unstable Wide-Complex Tachycardia — Monomorphic VT, Sedation, Synchronized Cardioversion

scenarioStory.history: 56-year-old female office manager was sitting at her desk reviewing payroll when she suddenly felt her heart 'racing and pounding hard.' Within minutes she became diaphoretic, developed substernal chest pressure rated 6/10, and felt severely lightheaded. A coworker noted her becoming pale and confused — answering questions slowly and asking the same question twice — and called 911. Patient has a history of an inferior STEMI 4 years ago (treated with primary PCI and a single stent to the RCA) and known left ventricular dysfunction (EF 35%) with documented non-sustained VT on a recent Holter, for which she was scheduled to see her electrophysiologist next week. She has been taking her metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily and amiodarone 200 mg daily as prescribed. Denies fever, recent illness, missed doses, recent stimulant or caffeine binge, or known electrolyte issues.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS finds a 56-year-old woman with known post-MI cardiomyopathy and prior NSVT lying on the office carpet, pale, diaphoretic, and visibly confused. Initial monitor shows a regular wide-complex tachycardia at 180 — monomorphic ventricular tachycardia. BP is 76/46 with chest pressure and altered mentation — this is unstable wide-complex tachycardia. The teaching point is rapid recognition that this patient bypasses the stable-VT pharmacologic ladder (amiodarone/procainamide/lidocaine) and goes directly to synchronized cardioversion with procedural sedation/analgesia. → Sedation given; sync cardioversion delivered: Crew briefly explains the procedure. Procedural sedation given: fentanyl 75 mcg IV (75 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 75 mcg, max 100 mcg) for analgesia, plus midazolam 2 mg IV slow push for amnesia/anxiolysis (small initial increment given marginal BP and elderly-leaning physiology). Pre-oxygenated, monitored, and synchronized cardioversion delivered at biphasic 100 J. Rhythm converts to sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with prompt restoration of BP and resolution of chest pain. Mentation clears within 30 seconds. → Sinus rhythm; transport to PCI/EP center: Patient transported to PCI- and electrophysiology-capable receiving hospital with continuous monitoring. Pads remain in place with the monitor pre-armed in sync mode for any VT recurrence. Continuous SpO2 and EtCO2 to detect post-sedation hypoventilation. Bedside handoff includes pre/post 12-leads, joules delivered, sedation given, and history of prior MI / EF 35% / recent NSVT.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Sudden palpitations, chest pressure, and progressive lightheadedness/confusion that began about 25 minutes ago while sitting at her desk; nearly slumped from her chair before coworkers caught her.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS finds a 56-year-old woman with known post-MI cardiomyopathy and prior NSVT lying on the office carpet, pale, diaphoretic, and visibly confused. Initial monitor shows a regular wide-complex tachycardia at 180 — monomorphic ventricular tachycardia. BP is 76/46 with chest pressure and altered mentation — this is unstable wide-complex tachycardia. The teaching point is rapid recognition that this patient bypasses the stable-VT pharmacologic ladder (amiodarone/procainamide/lidocaine) and goes directly to synchronized cardioversion with procedural sedation/analgesia.
relatedChecklist: Primary survey; identify instability — chest pain + AMS + hypotension = UNSTABLE
Cardiac monitor; defib pads on (sync mode pre-armed); 12-lead EKG
IV access ×2 large-bore proximal (sedation, post-conversion meds, fluids)
Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia driver
Classify: regular WIDE complex tachycardia + UNSTABLE → cardiovert (skip antiarrhythmic ladder)

scenarioEvents.name: Sedation given; sync cardioversion delivered
scenarioEvents.description: Crew briefly explains the procedure. Procedural sedation given: fentanyl 75 mcg IV (75 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 75 mcg, max 100 mcg) for analgesia, plus midazolam 2 mg IV slow push for amnesia/anxiolysis (small initial increment given marginal BP and elderly-leaning physiology). Pre-oxygenated, monitored, and synchronized cardioversion delivered at biphasic 100 J. Rhythm converts to sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with prompt restoration of BP and resolution of chest pain. Mentation clears within 30 seconds.
relatedChecklist: Fentanyl 75 mcg IV (75 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) — analgesia
Midazolam 2 mg slow IV — amnesia/anxiolysis (small increment given hypotension)
Allergies, BP, SpO2 confirmed; capnography on (earliest hypoventilation indicator)
Synchronized cardioversion 100 J biphasic; reassess pulse/rhythm post-shock
Post-shock: confirm rhythm, pulse, BP, mentation; rhythm strip + 12-lead

scenarioEvents.name: Sinus rhythm; transport to PCI/EP center
scenarioEvents.description: Patient transported to PCI- and electrophysiology-capable receiving hospital with continuous monitoring. Pads remain in place with the monitor pre-armed in sync mode for any VT recurrence. Continuous SpO2 and EtCO2 to detect post-sedation hypoventilation. Bedside handoff includes pre/post 12-leads, joules delivered, sedation given, and history of prior MI / EF 35% / recent NSVT.
relatedChecklist: Continuous monitor for VT recurrence; pads stay on; pre-arm sync mode
Continuous SpO2 + capnography during sedation wear-off; reassess RR/mentation q5 min
Transport to PCI/EP-capable hospital; bedside handoff with strips + dose timeline

scenarioEvents.name: Treated as STABLE — amiodarone given instead
scenarioEvents.description: Crew anchored on the wide-complex morphology and started amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes — a stable-VT treatment — without acknowledging the unstable signs (BP 76/46, AMS, chest pain). Three minutes into the infusion the rhythm degenerates from monomorphic VT into polymorphic VT, BP drops further, and the patient briefly loses consciousness. The teaching beat is that protocol explicitly defines unstable wide-complex VT as a cardioversion case, not an amiodarone case. The crew now must abandon the antiarrhythmic, deliver synchronized shock with what sedation they can give safely, and prepare for possible defibrillation if the rhythm becomes pulseless.
relatedChecklist: Stop amiodarone infusion; recognize misclassification — patient is UNSTABLE
Synchronized cardioversion 100 J biphasic immediately; pulse check after
BVM with O2 to support RR 8 and SpO2 86; suction ready; reposition airway
Pads in defib mode ready; if pulseless VT/VF, defibrillate per Cardiac Arrest protocol
Document misclassification; debrief: unstable WCT bypasses antiarrhythmic ladder
